ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No exact FoodEx2 code for Indian-style tomato salad with oil dressing; A00LC (mixed vegetable salad) used as closest parent analogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT food concept for tomato salad dish identified in available terminology.

serving.natural_language_portion: One typical side serving of tomato salad is approximately 100 g, consisting of sliced fresh tomatoes dressed with oil and seasoning.

nutrient_source.title: Tomato Salad — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from HPB Singapore Foodo record for Tomato Salad (cr_id G00010895). All per-100g values preserved from the canonical HPB source.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 38 is based on the University of Sydney GI Database value for raw tomato (GI ~38, low). The salad is predominantly tomato with an oil-based dressing; fat and protein in the dressing further suppress postprandial glucose response. GL per 100g serving = (38 × 5.9) / 100 = 2.24, rounded to 2.2.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 810 mg per 100 g — exceeds the 600 mg/100 g upper threshold for hypertension-safe foods
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should avoid this preparation; replacing the oil-based dressing with lemon juice and omitting added salt would reduce sodium to levels compatible with a hypertension-friendly diet.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate (5.9 g/100 g) and low GI (~38) are favourable, but very high fat content (44.54 g/100 g) from oil-based dressing adds significant caloric load
type2_diabetes.guidance: The tomato base is suitable for blood glucose management due to low carbohydrate and low GI; however, patients with Type 2 Diabetes should request dressing on the side or substitute with lemon juice to limit excess caloric and fat intake.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High total fat (44.54 g/100 g), estimated saturated fat (~7.5 g/100 g), cholesterol (26 mg/100 g), and sodium (810 mg/100 g) collectively elevate cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should avoid this high-fat, high-sodium preparation and opt for a plain tomato salad dressed only with lemon juice to benefit from lycopene and potassium without the adverse lipid and sodium load.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Nightshade vegetable (solanine sensitivity risk), saturated fat ~7.5 g/100 g exceeds 5 g RA threshold, and sodium 810 mg/100 g promotes tissue swelling
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: RA patients should monitor individual nightshade sensitivity to tomato, replace the heavy oil-based dressing with extra-virgin olive oil and lemon juice to reduce saturated fat and sodium, and limit portion size.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Tomato Salad (Indian style, Singapore) is a minimally processed vegetable dish consisting of fresh sliced tomatoes dressed with an oil-based dressing and seasoning, commonly served as a side dish at Indian meals in Singapore. The tomato base provides lycopene, potassium (64 mg/100 g), and calcium (28 mg/100 g), and contributes low carbohydrate (5.9 g/100 g) and low sugar (4.69 g/100 g). However, the heavy oil-based dressing dramatically elevates the fat content to 44.54 g/100 g and energy to 430 kcal/100 g, making this a calorie-dense preparation despite its vegetable base. Sodium is very high at 810 mg/100 g due to added salt and seasoning in the dressing, making this unsuitable for hypertension and cardiovascular disease patients in its current form. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 38 (low), based on the tomato base, and the fat content further blunts postprandial glucose response. Dietary fibre is reported as 0.0 g/100 g per the HPB source. Cholesterol is 26 mg/100 g, likely from egg-based or dairy-based dressing components. A plain version with lemon juice dressing would be significantly healthier across all clinical conditions.
common_user_queries: Is tomato salad good for diabetes?
Is tomato salad suitable for high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the canonical HPB Singapore Foodo database record (cr_id G00010895, confidence 0.95, review_status reviewed). All core macros and secondary nutrients are present from this approved primary source.
notes: Saturated fat is estimated at ~7.5 g/100 g based on the fat profile of a typical oil-based salad dressing (referenced in health_suitability from the input record); the HPB source does not separately report saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, or trans fat — these are set to -1 except saturated_fat_g which is estimated. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, vitamin panel, and moisture data for this specific Indian-style tomato salad preparation are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. Dietary_fibre_g is reported as 0.0 g/100 g per the HPB source; this is confirmed zero per the canonical HPB record and is noted as unusually low for a tomato-based dish — likely reflecting the specific preparation with heavy dressing diluting fibre concentration. GI is an analogous estimate based on raw tomato GI (~38) from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for this specific Indian-style tomato salad preparation exists in the approved GI database. Cultural classification as Indian cuisine is preserved from the input record; tomato salad is a common side dish in Indian cuisine served in Singapore.
